unit.name: Problem formulation for robot learning
unit.description: Defines robot learning problems using goals, states, actions, rewards, and physical-world feedback, then grounds the abstraction with examples from control, games, language, chatbots, and cloth folding.
unit.summary: Li formalizes robot learning with an agent that receives a task objective, observes state from the physical world, takes an action, and receives both the next state and reward [ts=345s] [ts=373s]. He stresses that, unlike much of computer vision, robotics is framed as optimization under physical constraints: the system must choose actions that improve an objective in the real world [ts=405s] [ts=416s]. The segment walks through cart-pole, robot locomotion, Atari, Go, text generation, chatbots, and cloth folding to show that the same goal-state-action-reward template applies broadly [ts=442s] [ts=476s] [ts=505s] [ts=596s] [ts=631s]. It closes by noting that reward design is application-specific, such as balancing speed versus comfort in driving or compactness versus smoothness in cloth folding [ts=705s] [ts=720s] [ts=742s].

key_points.text: A robot learning problem is specified by goal, state, action, reward, and the resulting next state.
Robotics is posed as optimization in a physical world rather than only representation learning from high-dimensional inputs.
Cart-pole uses pole angle and cart state as inputs, horizontal force as action, and uprightness as reward.
Robot locomotion uses joint states, torques, and forward/upright reward.
Chatbots can also be cast as sequential decision making with conversation state, next-sentence action, and human-evaluation reward.
Reward design is nuanced and must match the application, such as comfort in driving or smoothness in cloth folding.

kp_catalog.name: Goal-state-action-reward formulation
kp_catalog.description: Robot learning problems are structured around a task goal, current state, action choice, reward signal, and resulting next state.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because goal-state-action-reward formulation formalizes how robot learning problems are structured around a task goal, current state, action choice, reward signal, and resulting next state, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Reward design and task specification
kp_catalog.description: Reward functions are not uniquely determined by the task name; they encode the specific preferences and metrics that define success in an application.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because reward design and task specification clarifies reward functions are not uniquely determined by the task name; they encode the specific preferences and metrics that define success in an application, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Robot learning as interactive sequential decision-making
kp_catalog.description: Robot learning is distinguished from standard prediction problems because actions change the environment, generate new observations, and must be chosen over time to improve reward.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because robot learning as interactive sequential decision-making clarifies robot learning is distinguished from standard prediction problems because actions change the environment, generate new observations, and must be chosen over time to improve reward, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
